comment_text.sn4.14:1.1: Ekasālā (“One Hall”) is mentioned only here in Pali, although a village of the same name is occasionally mention in Sanskrit and epigraphical sources. Compare the similarly-named villages of Sālā (MN 41:1.2, etc.) and Pañcasālā (SN 4.18:1.1).
comment_text.sn4.14:3.1: The challenge and response are similar to SN 10.2.
comment_text.sn4.14:3.4: Māra is projecting. Since he only uses words to manipulate people and get what he wants, he assumes that others do the same.

root_text.sn4.14:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā kosalesu viharati ekasālāyaṁ brāhmaṇagāme.
root_text.sn4.14:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena bhagavā mahatiyā gihiparisāya parivuto dhammaṁ deseti.
root_text.sn4.14:2.2: “ayaṁ kho samaṇo gotamo mahatiyā gihiparisāya parivuto dhammaṁ deseti.
root_text.sn4.14:2.3: Yannūnāhaṁ yena samaṇo gotamo tenupasaṅkameyyaṁ vicakkhukammāyā”ti.
root_text.sn4.14:2.4: Atha kho māro pāpimā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn4.14:5.1: Atha kho māro pāpimā “jānāti maṁ bhagavā, jānāti maṁ sugato”ti dukkhī dummano tatthevantaradhāyīti.

translation_text.sn4.14:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Kosalans near the brahmin village of Ekasālā.
translation_text.sn4.14:1.2: Now, at that time the Buddha was teaching Dhamma, surrounded by a large assembly of laypeople.
translation_text.sn4.14:2.2: “The ascetic Gotama is teaching Dhamma, surrounded by a large assembly of laypeople.
translation_text.sn4.14:2.3: Why don’t I go and pull the wool over their eyes?”
translation_text.sn4.14:2.4: Then Māra the Wicked went up to the Buddha and addressed him in verse:
translation_text.sn4.14:3.4: don’t get caught up in favoring and opposing.”
translation_text.sn4.14:4.2: out of compassion for their welfare.
translation_text.sn4.14:5.1: Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there.
